options.content:
**The system can be measured two ways — and the contrast is the point.**

- **RAW BENCHMARK PATH** (model/batcher direct, *no LLM*): raw ViT throughput — the engine's *capacity* (the assignment's "max model QPS"). ~30-50 QPS, latency in **ms**. Fed by `just bench-qps` / `bench-prod`.
- **USER PATH** (`/chat`): the full shopper pipeline (chat → orchestrator → embed → retrieval → LLM) — what the shopper *feels*. LLM-bound: ~1 QPS, latency in **seconds**. Fed by real `/chat` traffic (browser sessions and E2E test runs).

**The gap between them is the story:** the engine is fast (ms), but felt latency is LLM-dominated (s). All-metrics ops view: **[avsa-detail](/d/avsa-detail)**.

panels.title: [USER] Conversation p95 (SLO 3000 ms)
panels.description: USER PATH. End-to-end conversation-turn p95 latency from avsa_conversation_latency_seconds (event [:avsa, :conversation, :complete]).  SLO: p95 <= 3000 ms. This is what the shopper feels — if it climbs, the hero decomposition at the bottom tells you which stage is to blame (it is almost always the LLM/attribute step).

panels.title: [USER] Chat success rate
panels.description: USER PATH. Fraction of conversation turns that succeeded, from avsa_chat_outcome_total{outcome} (event [:avsa, :conversation, :complete]). 1.0 = every turn produced a verified answer; a dip means turns are erroring (cross-check the verifier panel in avsa-detail).

panels.title: [RAW] Raw model QPS (live)
panels.description: RAW BENCHMARK PATH. Live raw ViT /embed throughput measured directly at the model service (job=avsa-model, :8090) — no LLM in the loop. This is the engine's capacity (the assignment's "max model QPS"). Thresholds are the T4 fp32 acceptance bars from config/avsa.toml [bench.targets]: raw_model_qps_min=60 (yellow), system_qps_min=150 (green) — . Reads ~0 until `just bench-qps` / `bench-prod` runs — that is expected, not a broken panel.

panels.title: [RAW] Raw embed p95
panels.description: RAW BENCHMARK PATH. p95 latency of the raw ViT /embed call measured directly at the model service (job=avsa-model, :8090) from http_request_duration_seconds — no LLM in the loop. Expect single-digit-to-tens of ms: this is the engine's true cost. Contrast with the USER PATH conversation p95 (seconds) to the left — that is the gap the thesis is about. Reads no data until `just bench-qps` / `bench-prod` runs.

options.content:
**OFFLINE — not live.** recall@5 / QPS frontier from `evals/`, refreshed by `just capture-baseline`.

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **recall@5 image** | **~0.48** ⚠ |
| recall@5 text | 0.99 |
| QPS raw / system | 33 / 47 |
| T4 target raw / sys | 60 / 150 |

**recall@5 image ~0.48 is the honest weakness** the CLIP swap targets — the ViT image tower clusters by visual category, not product identity. Sources: `evals/qps/baseline/baseline.json` (`capture_telemetry.recall`, `summary`), `evals/retrieval/{image,text}/baseline.toml`, `config/avsa.toml [bench.targets]`.

panels.title: USER PATH (/chat — fed by real chat traffic)
[USER] Conversation Latency — p50/p95/p99 (SLO 3000 ms)
panels.description: USER PATH. End-to-end conversation turn latency from avsa_conversation_latency_seconds (event [:avsa, :conversation, :complete]) over time.  SLO: p95 <= 3000 ms (red threshold line). This is the trend behind the scoreboard p95 stat — everything else exists to explain this curve. Fed by real `/chat` traffic.

panels.title: [USER] ViT /embed QPS — chat/orchestrator counter (T4  targets)
panels.description: USER PATH. ViT embedding throughput on the conversation path from avsa_vit_qps_total (event [:avsa, :embed, :complete], orchestrator :9568). This is the chat/orchestrator embed counter — it only moves when real `/chat` traffic runs, NOT during a raw bench (that drives the avsa-model :8090 counter in the RAW section below). Reference lines are the T4 fp32 acceptance bars from config/avsa.toml [bench.targets]: raw_model_qps_min=60, system_qps_min=150. These are GPU targets — local CPU/MPS will sit below them by design. ViT throughput = shoppers served per GPU; higher QPS lowers cost-per-shopper, not felt latency.

panels.title: RAW BENCHMARK PATH (model/batcher — fed by bench-qps/bench-prod)
[RAW] Raw model QPS — /embed @ avsa-model :8090 (T4 60/150 targets)
panels.description: RAW BENCHMARK PATH. Raw ViT /embed throughput measured directly at the model service (job=avsa-model, :8090) from http_requests_total{handler="/embed"} — no LLM in the loop. This is the engine's capacity (the assignment's "max model QPS"). Reference lines are the T4 fp32 acceptance bars from config/avsa.toml [bench.targets]: raw_model_qps_min=60, system_qps_min=150. Reads ~0 until `just bench-qps` / `bench-prod` runs — that is expected, not a broken panel.

panels.title: [RAW] Raw embed latency — p50/p95/p99 @ avsa-model :8090
panels.description: RAW BENCHMARK PATH. p50/p95/p99 latency of the raw ViT /embed call at the model service (job=avsa-model, :8090) from http_request_duration_seconds — no LLM in the loop. Expect ms-scale: this is the engine's true cost, the contrast against the seconds-scale USER PATH conversation latency. Reads no data until `just bench-qps` / `bench-prod` runs.

panels.title: [RAW] Batcher flush latency p95
panels.description: RAW BENCHMARK PATH. p95 of the dynamic-batcher flush latency from avsa_batcher_flush_latency_seconds — how long a batch waits before the model processes it. This is the throughput knob behind raw QPS: tighter flushes trade latency for batch fullness. Moves under raw bench load (`just bench-qps` / `bench-prod`).

panels.title: Latency Decomposition — where the wait goes (ViT embed vs Retrieval vs LLM/Attribute, p95)
panels.description: THE BRIDGE between the two paths. p95 of each USER-PATH conversation stage on one axis. ViT embed: avsa_embed_latency_seconds (~tens of ms — matches the RAW embed latency above). Retrieval (pgvector kNN): avsa_orch_tool_retrieval_duration_seconds (~tens of ms). LLM/attribute extraction: avsa_orch_tool_attribute_duration_seconds (~1-3 s, dominates). Takeaway: the raw engine is fast (ms), but felt latency is LLM-bound — making ViT faster buys capacity/cost-per-shopper, not felt latency; to cut felt latency, attack the LLM/attribute stage.
